title: Unnecessary small jogs in otherwise straight horizontal strand routes
summary: The image shows several fiber strand routes that could be drawn as straight horizontal runs, but instead include small vertical jogs before continuing horizontally. During automatic layout, cable and fan-out positions should be nudged enough for these near-horizontal strands to snap to straight lanes when doing so does not violate higher-priority routing rules.
visually_observable_issues: Multiple colored fiber strand routes are nearly horizontal but include a small vertical step before continuing straight.
The jogs appear avoidable because the strand segments before and after the jog are parallel and aligned close to a valid straight horizontal lane.
The stepped shape makes the routing look less clean even though there is no visible obstacle forcing the detour in the cropped screenshot.
The repeated small jog pattern suggests the auto layout did not nudge the adjacent cable or fan-out positions enough to align route lanes.

broken_requirements: During automatic layout, cable and fan-out positions should be nudged so near-horizontal or near-vertical strand runs become straight.
Tiny unnecessary jogs must not remain when a straight run is feasible without violating higher-priority hard rules.
Spacing should leave labels, fanouts, groups, and individual strands visually separated and traceable without avoidable congestion.
likely_broken_rules.why_this_image_fails: The visible strands have small stepped jogs even though the route intent appears to be a straight horizontal run. The layout should shift cable placement or fan-out positions just enough for the affected strands to align to straight lanes.

broken_requirements: The routing engine should use available horizontal and vertical space.
The routing engine should avoid unnecessary congestion near splice dots and through the routing zone.
A route fails when it ignores available spacing and creates avoidable congestion.
likely_broken_rules.why_this_image_fails: The small jogs use extra route geometry in the routing zone where a cleaner straight horizontal run appears available.

broken_requirements: Fan-out exit points should align to valid grid lanes where possible.
Fan-out exit points must allow minimum bend clearance inside the routing zone.
The fan-out provides clean start points for the routing engine.
likely_broken_rules.why_this_image_fails: The user-described correction depends on nudging fiber fan-outs up or down so their exit points align with straight horizontal route lanes.

likely_broken_rules.rule_name: Fiber Strand Overlap, Crossing, and Collision
broken_requirements: Routes must remain visually traceable from fan-out exit to fusion splice dot.
Routes must avoid unnecessary crossing and route interference.
A path fails when it ignores an available cleaner path.
likely_broken_rules.why_this_image_fails: Even without visible overlap in this crop, the stepped routes are lower-quality than available straight paths and make strand routing less visually direct.

expected_correction: When a strand can be routed horizontally in a straight line, draw it as a straight horizontal run.
Auto layout should shift the fiber cables on either side or nudge the fiber fan-outs up or down just enough that the affected strands snap to straight lanes.
Keep the correction subordinate to hard constraints such as label avoidance, fan-out clearance, reserved routing zones, strand spacing, and collision avoidance.
notes: This should be treated as a bad example of avoidable route geometry rather than a CSV data-shape issue.

The screenshot is cropped, so any hidden off-crop obstacle cannot be ruled out from the image alone. The user-reported defect and the visible repeated jog pattern support high confidence that the jogs are unnecessary.
This example is closely tied to the near-straight alignment requirement in SDC-LAYOUT-001.
